enrichment.short_description: Feeds Fun is an open-source RSS reader that uses AI-based tagging and user-defined scoring rules to automatically rank and filter incoming feed entries.
enrichment.seo_title: Feeds Fun Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Feeds Fun review: an open-source, AI-tagging RSS reader that scores and filters feed entries automatically. See features, self-hosting steps, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Feeds Fun is an open-source, self-hostable RSS and Atom feed reader designed to solve a specific problem: information overload for people who subscribe to hundreds of feeds and receive far more stories per day than they can realistically read. Rather than presenting a raw, chronological firehose of headlines the way traditional readers do, Feeds Fun automatically assigns descriptive tags to every incoming entry using a system of pluggable 'tag processors,' and then lets the user write simple scoring rules over those tags, such as adding or subtracting points when a tag appears, so the application can automatically rank, highlight, or hide stories based on the reader's own stated interests. The most distinctive tag processor is an LLM-based one, internally named llm_general, which calls out to a large language model provider, either OpenAI's ChatGPT models or Google's Gemini models, using an API key supplied by the user, to read each entry and generate a set of relevant topical tags. Because the AI tagging step is bring-your-own-key, the cost of running the LLM calls is borne directly by the user rather than by the Feeds Fun project itself, which keeps the core product free to operate at any scale the user is willing to pay their own AI provider for.

Feeds Fun was created by a developer who goes by the handle Tiendil (Aliaksei Yaletski), a self-described indie game developer and hacker based in Germany. It is not a venture-backed company or even a small team effort; it is, by all available evidence, a solo open-source side project maintained by a single person, with GitHub's contributor statistics showing effectively one contributor responsible for the overwhelming majority of commits. The project's GitHub repository was created in March 2023, and the creator published a public 'Hello World' introduction post in August 2024 explaining the motivation: he was personally following around five hundred RSS feeds, receiving somewhere between five hundred and a thousand new stories per day, and could not find an existing open-source reader that filtered content intelligently enough to match his needs, so he built his own using large language models to do the heavy lifting of relevance judgment. He has stated that using the tool himself saves him something on the order of four to eight hours per week that would otherwise be spent manually skimming headlines. There is no registered company behind Feeds Fun, no Crunchbase listing, no LinkedIn company page, and no venture funding of any kind; it exists purely as a piece of software released under the permissive BSD 3-Clause license and distributed both as a hosted instance at feeds.fun and as a self-hostable Docker deployment.

Technically, Feeds Fun is built as a Python backend, distributed as the PyPI package ffun, running as an ASGI application under uvicorn with separate background worker processes handling feed loading and the 'librarian' tagging pipeline, backed by a PostgreSQL database for persistence. The frontend is a Vue and TypeScript single-page application, published as the npm package feeds-fun, that talks to the backend API. Anyone wanting to run their own instance can do so with Docker Compose, using either a 'single-user' profile for a personal deployment or a 'multi-user' profile intended for small groups sharing one server. Because the AI tagging integration is built against the OpenAI and Gemini API formats but is not hard-coded exclusively to those two vendors, users can in principle point it at any compatible API endpoint, including some locally hosted or third-party OpenAI-compatible inference servers, which gives privacy-conscious or cost-conscious users more flexibility than a closed SaaS RSS reader would.

Feeds Fun is aimed squarely at power users of RSS: people who already understand the concept of feed subscriptions and who are drowning in volume rather than starved for content. It is not trying to be a mass-market consumer news app, and it has no social features, no algorithmic 'For You' timeline in the social-media sense, and no advertising business model. Instead its value proposition is closer to that of a personal research assistant layered on top of a conventional feed aggregator: the tags and scoring rules act like a lightweight, user-controlled recommendation engine, and because the rules are transparent and editable rather than a black-box algorithm, users retain full control over what gets surfaced.

Within the broader landscape of RSS readers, Feeds Fun sits in the self-hosted, open-source category alongside tools such as FreshRSS, Miniflux, Tiny Tiny RSS, CommaFeed, and Selfoss, all of which appear together on curated self-hosting lists such as Awesome-Selfhosted. Feeds Fun has not published any direct comparison content against these tools, and there is no independent third-party benchmark pitting it against commercial cloud readers like Feedly, Inoreader, or NewsBlur, but its core differentiator relative to essentially all of these alternatives is the built-in LLM-driven auto-tagging and rule-based scoring system, which none of the mainstream open-source readers offer out of the box in the same integrated way. Most competing self-hosted readers focus on reliable feed parsing, a clean reading interface, and basic keyword filters, whereas Feeds Fun's central feature is specifically the AI relevance layer.

As of the most recent check, the project's GitHub repository had accumulated a modest but real following, with a few hundred stars, a small number of forks, and dozens of open issues, alongside two low-engagement Show HN style posts on Hacker News. It has no Wikipedia page and has not received coverage from major technology press, which is consistent with its profile as a niche, community-discovered tool rather than a marketed product. The project maintains an active in-repository changelog documenting a steady cadence of releases, indicating ongoing maintenance rather than an abandoned experiment. For a technically inclined user who already runs a home server or a small VPS, is comfortable with Docker, and wants a genuinely open, inspectable alternative to commercial RSS readers with AI features bolted on, Feeds Fun offers a narrow but well-executed solution: bring your own OpenAI or Gemini API key, define your interests as scoring rules over automatically generated tags, and let the system quietly triage a large feed volume down to what actually matters to you, all without handing your reading habits or feed list to a third-party SaaS company.

Looking at Feeds Fun's broader technical architecture, the separation of concerns between the loader process, which fetches and parses raw RSS and Atom feed data, and the librarian process, which orchestrates tag processors including the LLM-based tagger, reflects a deliberate design choice to keep ingestion and enrichment decoupled, so that a slow or rate-limited AI provider call does not block the core feed-fetching pipeline. This separation also means the system degrades gracefully: if a user has not configured an AI API key, or if that key runs out of quota, the deterministic tag processors and manual scoring rules continue to function, and only the AI-enriched tagging layer is unavailable, rather than the entire application failing. From a market-position standpoint, Feeds Fun occupies a genuinely narrow niche at the intersection of two much larger categories, self-hosted RSS readers and AI-assisted content curation tools, and it has not attempted to expand outward into either adjacent space, such as adding social sharing, newsletter publishing, or a hosted freemium business model with paid tiers, the way some larger commercial readers have. Its product evolution since the 2023 repository creation has been steady but incremental, adding refinements such as improved tag normalization, expanded LLM provider compatibility, and ongoing changelog entries through 2026, rather than any dramatic pivot in scope or business model, consistent with its identity as a personally motivated tool that its creator continues to refine primarily for his own use and shares openly with anyone who finds it useful.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Feeds Fun is a free, open-source RSS and Atom reader built around one core idea: instead of showing every subscribed feed as one long chronological list, it uses AI-generated tags and user-written scoring rules to automatically surface the entries that matter most to a given reader and quietly demote or hide the rest.

It was created and is maintained by a single independent developer rather than a company, and it is distributed under the permissive BSD 3-Clause license, with both a public hosted instance and a self-hostable Docker deployment available to anyone who wants full control over their data and infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The heart of Feeds Fun is its 'tag processor' architecture, which includes simple deterministic processors, such as tagging entries by their source domain or importing tags already present in the original feed, alongside an LLM-based processor that sends entry content to OpenAI or Google Gemini models to generate rich, topical tags automatically.

On top of these tags, users define scoring rules, for example adding points when a certain tag is present and subtracting points for another, which the application uses to continuously rank incoming stories, effectively building a personalized, transparent recommendation system rather than a black-box algorithmic feed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Feeds Fun itself is entirely free, whether used via the hosted instance at feeds.fun or self-hosted through Docker Compose, and there is no paid subscription tier for the software.

The only cost a user might incur is indirect: because the AI tagging feature calls OpenAI's or Google Gemini's APIs using the user's own API key, any usage charges for those AI calls are billed directly by the AI provider to the user, separate from Feeds Fun itself.

features.title: AI-powered tagging
features.description: An LLM-based tag processor calls OpenAI or Google Gemini models using the user's own API key to automatically generate relevant topic tags for every feed entry.

features.title: Rule-based scoring
features.description: Users write simple scoring rules over tags, adding or subtracting points, to automatically rank and prioritize which stories rise to the top of their feed.

features.title: Self-hosting via Docker
features.description: Feeds Fun ships as a Docker Compose deployment with single-user and multi-user profiles, giving technical users full control over their own instance and data.

features.title: PostgreSQL-backed storage
features.description: All feed data, tags, and scoring history are persisted in a PostgreSQL database, making the deployment straightforward to back up, inspect, and query directly.

features.title: Deterministic tag processors
features.description: In addition to AI tagging, simple processors can tag entries by source domain or import tags natively present in the original RSS or Atom feed.

features.title: Bring-your-own AI key
features.description: AI tagging costs are billed directly to the user's own OpenAI or Gemini account, keeping the core Feeds Fun software free regardless of usage volume.

features.title: Vue and TypeScript frontend
features.description: A modern single-page application frontend, distributed as the npm package feeds-fun, provides the reading and rule-configuration interface.

features.title: Compatible API endpoints
features.description: Because the AI integration follows the OpenAI and Gemini API formats, users can point Feeds Fun at other compatible endpoints, including some self-hosted inference servers.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive BSD 3-Clause license
AI-driven tagging genuinely reduces manual triage time for heavy RSS users
Transparent, user-controlled scoring rules instead of an opaque recommendation algorithm
Self-hostable with Docker for full data ownership and privacy
cons: Requires a personal OpenAI or Gemini API key and technical comfort with Docker and PostgreSQL for self-hosting
Maintained by a single developer, so long-term support and roadmap continuity carry more risk than a funded company
No official mobile apps; access is via the web interface
Minimal documentation and no enterprise or team-collaboration features

faqs.question: Is Feeds Fun free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Feeds Fun is fully open source and free, whether you use the hosted instance at feeds.fun or self-host it yourself; the only potential cost is usage fees from your own OpenAI or Gemini API key for AI tagging.

faqs.question: Who makes Feeds Fun?
faqs.answer: Feeds Fun is created and maintained by an independent developer known online as Tiendil, based in Germany; it is a solo open-source project rather than a venture-backed company.

faqs.question: Do I need an OpenAI or Gemini account to use it?
faqs.answer: You only need one if you want to use the AI-powered tagging feature; the rest of the reader, including feed subscriptions and manual tags, works without an AI key.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Feeds Fun?
faqs.answer: Yes, Feeds Fun can be self-hosted via Docker Compose with a single-user profile for personal use or a multi-user profile for small groups, and it requires a PostgreSQL database.

faqs.question: What license is Feeds Fun released under?
faqs.answer: Feeds Fun is released under the BSD 3-Clause license, a permissive open-source license that allows free use, modification, and redistribution.

faqs.question: How is Feeds Fun different from Feedly or Inoreader?
faqs.answer: Unlike commercial cloud readers, Feeds Fun is free, open source, and self-hostable, and its core differentiator is a built-in LLM-based tagging and rule-scoring system for automatically ranking entries rather than relying on manual folders or keyword filters alone.

use_cases.title: Taming a large RSS subscription list
use_cases.description: Readers following hundreds of feeds use AI tagging and scoring rules to automatically surface only the most relevant stories instead of scrolling an unfiltered chronological list.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious self-hosted news reading
use_cases.description: Technical users who do not want a commercial SaaS company tracking their reading habits deploy Feeds Fun on their own server via Docker for full data ownership.

use_cases.title: Topic-focused monitoring
use_cases.description: Users interested in tracking specific topics across many sources define scoring rules around relevant tags so that on-topic stories are automatically promoted.

features: Self-hosted or hosted use at feeds.fun
AI-powered tagging via your own OpenAI or Gemini API key

enrichment.short_description: Fern is a developer platform that generates type-safe SDKs and API documentation websites directly from an OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, or Protobuf specification.
enrichment.seo_title: Fern Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fern review: generate type-safe SDKs and API docs from an OpenAPI spec. Pricing, key features, and how it compares to Mintlify, Stainless, and ReadMe.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fern is a developer-experience platform built around a simple but powerful premise summarized in its own tagline: input an OpenAPI specification, output SDKs and documentation. Rather than requiring API-first companies to hand-write and separately maintain client libraries in every language their customers might want, plus a documentation site that describes those same endpoints, Fern ingests a single machine-readable API definition, which can be an OpenAPI or AsyncAPI spec, a GraphQL schema, a Protobuf or gRPC definition, or an OpenRPC document, and generates both a matched set of type-safe client SDKs and a fully built documentation website from that one source of truth. The generated SDKs are not thin, mechanically produced wrappers; Fern's generators add real ergonomics on top of raw API calls, including built-in authentication handling, automatic retries with backoff, pagination helpers that let developers iterate over paginated resources without manually managing cursors, and support for streaming responses over Server-Sent Events or WebSockets. The accompanying documentation sites are similarly full-featured, offering an automatically generated API reference that stays in sync with the underlying spec, versioning support for multiple API versions, built-in search, an AI chat assistant that can answer questions about the documented API, support for llms.txt and Model Context Protocol integration so that AI coding assistants can consume the documentation programmatically, and custom domain hosting.

Fern was founded in 2022 in Brooklyn, New York, by Danny Sheridan, who serves as CEO, and Deep Singhvi, who serves as CTO; a third co-founder, Zach Kirsch, is listed by at least one secondary source though this has not been independently confirmed by Fern's own materials. The company went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 batch, which provided its earliest institutional backing. Fern subsequently raised a $500,000 seed round in April 2023 led by Y Combinator, followed by a $9 million Series A in April 2025 led by Bessemer Venture Partners with continued participation from Y Combinator. Press coverage has repeatedly cited a headline figure of roughly $13 million in total funding, a number that does not cleanly reconcile against the two publicly itemized rounds, which sum to closer to $9.5 million; Crunchbase records additional unnamed seed investors including Ironwood Ventures, suggesting the seed round itself may have been larger than the $500,000 figure most commonly cited, though the exact reconciliation is not fully documented in public sources. No formal valuation for either round has been disclosed.

By the time of a major milestone in January 2026, Fern had grown to roughly twenty-five employees, though a LinkedIn company page separately listed the company in the eleven-to-fifty employee band, which is broadly consistent. That milestone was Fern's acquisition by Postman, the widely used API platform company, announced January 8, 2026, with deal terms undisclosed. Under the acquisition, Fern's full team, including both co-founders, joined Postman, and Fern has continued to operate as what Postman describes as an independent product within its broader portfolio rather than being immediately absorbed and rebranded. This means that Fern's pricing, roadmap, and organizational structure as described in most publicly available materials reflect its last known state as an independently operated company, and some of these details may evolve now that it sits inside a larger, well-capitalized parent organization with its own established API tooling business.

Before the acquisition, Fern's pricing followed a three-tier structure published on its own pricing page. The Hobby tier was free and included support for two team members, two hundred fifty AI credits, a custom domain, and password-protected documentation, making it usable by solo developers or very small projects experimenting with the platform. The Team tier cost one hundred fifty dollars per month and expanded capacity to five team members, one thousand AI credits, a custom subpath for documentation, version history support, and PDF export of documentation content. The Enterprise tier moved to custom, quote-based pricing negotiated through a sales demo, and unlocked SSO and JWT-based authentication, role-based access control, a self-hosted generation option for companies that need to keep their SDK build pipeline inside their own infrastructure, and dedicated support. This flat, seat-and-credit-based monthly pricing structure, rather than a usage-metered model tied to API call volume, was one of the more distinctive aspects of how Fern packaged its product relative to some developer-tool competitors.

Fern's target customer is fundamentally any API-first company or developer platform that ships a public or partner-facing API and needs to give external developers a good integration experience without dedicating a large internal team to hand-maintaining SDKs across many languages. Its own customer list, drawn from published case studies, includes a number of recognizable technology companies such as ElevenLabs, Auth0, Webflow, Deepgram, Deel, NVIDIA, Square, and Twilio, and Adobe's Frame.io product, along with OpenRouter, spanning both younger API-native startups and established enterprise platforms. This customer mix signals that Fern's appeal extends from small teams needing to bootstrap a professional developer experience quickly all the way to larger organizations replacing bespoke, internally maintained SDK generation pipelines.

Within its competitive category, Fern is most frequently compared against Mintlify, Stainless, Speakeasy, and ReadMe, and the company has published direct head-to-head comparison content against each of these four rivals on its own site. Fern's own framing of its differentiation centers on offering a unified pipeline that produces both SDKs and documentation from the same specification, with documentation code snippets sourced directly from the real generated SDK code rather than hand-written or separately maintained examples, plus support for a broader range of input specification formats than some competitors and the option to self-host the generation process for security-sensitive enterprise customers. Stainless and Speakeasy occupy similar territory as SDK-generation-first companies, while Mintlify and ReadMe have historically been more documentation-hosting-first, with Fern positioning itself at the intersection of both categories rather than choosing one specialization. No public comparison from Fern against Redocly or Scalar, two other API documentation tools, was found, suggesting those may be viewed as more adjacent than directly competitive.

Technically, Fern is operated through a command-line interface with a small number of core commands: fern init to scaffold a new Fern workspace pointed at an existing API specification, fern check to validate that specification against Fern's requirements, and fern generate to produce the actual SDKs and documentation output. This CLI-driven workflow is designed to slot into existing continuous integration pipelines, so that whenever a company updates its OpenAPI specification as part of normal API development, the corresponding SDKs and documentation can be regenerated and published automatically rather than requiring a separate manual step, which is one of the core promises of the product: keeping documentation and client libraries permanently synchronized with the actual behavior of the API rather than letting them drift out of date over time, a common and costly problem for growing API businesses.

From an architectural standpoint, Fern's generator system is built to treat each target language as a separate, independently versioned code-generation pipeline that consumes the same intermediate representation of the API definition, which is why Fern can add support for new languages or update an existing language's generated code style without requiring changes to how a company's underlying OpenAPI specification is written. This design also underlies Fern's documentation-snippet accuracy claim: because the code samples shown in the documentation site are derived from the same generator output used to produce the real SDKs, rather than maintained as separate hand-written examples, the two artifacts cannot easily drift out of sync with each other, which is a common failure mode for API documentation maintained through more manual processes. In terms of market position, Fern entered a developer-tools category that grew rapidly during the early 2020s as more companies shifted to an API-first go-to-market motion, competing for mindshare against both well-funded, venture-backed rivals like Speakeasy and Stainless and more documentation-centric incumbents like ReadMe and Mintlify; its acquisition by Postman in January 2026 represents a notable consolidation move in that category, with Postman, an already dominant player in API development and testing tooling, absorbing a company whose core competency, SDK and documentation generation, complements rather than duplicates Postman's existing product surface, suggesting the acquisition was likely motivated by a desire to round out Postman's platform rather than to eliminate a direct competitor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Fern is a developer platform that turns a single API specification, such as an OpenAPI or AsyncAPI document, into type-safe client SDKs across multiple programming languages plus a matching API documentation website, so API-first companies do not have to hand-build and separately maintain both.
Founded in 2022 in Brooklyn and grown through Y Combinator, Fern was acquired by Postman in January 2026 and now continues operating as an independent product inside Postman's broader API platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Fern's generated SDKs include real production-grade ergonomics beyond raw API wrappers, such as built-in authentication handling, automatic retries, pagination helpers, and support for streaming over Server-Sent Events or WebSockets, across languages including TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, C#, Swift, and Rust.

The accompanying documentation sites include an always-in-sync API reference, versioning, search, an AI chat assistant, llms.txt and Model Context Protocol support for AI coding tools, and custom domain hosting, all generated from the same source specification used to build the SDKs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Fern's last publicly listed pricing, prior to its 2026 acquisition by Postman, offered three tiers: a free Hobby tier for small projects, a Team tier at one hundred fifty dollars per month for growing teams, and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier with SSO, RBAC, and self-hosted generation for larger organizations.

Pricing is structured around flat monthly fees tied to team members and AI credits rather than metered by API call or SDK download volume, though pricing details may evolve now that Fern operates within Postman.

faqs.question: What does Fern do?
faqs.answer: Fern takes an OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, GraphQL, Protobuf, or OpenRPC specification and generates type-safe client SDKs in multiple programming languages along with a matching API documentation website from that same specification.

faqs.question: Is Fern free to use?
faqs.answer: Fern offers a free Hobby tier for small projects with limited team members and AI credits, alongside paid Team and custom-priced Enterprise tiers for larger teams and organizations.

faqs.question: Which languages can Fern generate SDKs for?
faqs.answer: Fern's generators cover languages including TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, C#, Swift, and Rust, generated from a single underlying API specification.

faqs.question: Was Fern acquired?
faqs.answer: Yes, Fern was acquired by Postman in January 2026, with the full Fern team joining Postman while Fern continues to operate as an independent product.

faqs.question: How is Fern different from Mintlify or ReadMe?
faqs.answer: Fern differentiates itself by generating both SDKs and documentation from one specification, with documentation code snippets sourced directly from the real generated SDK code, while Mintlify and ReadMe have historically focused more narrowly on documentation hosting.

faqs.question: Can Fern be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Enterprise customers can run Fern's SDK and documentation generation pipeline within their own infrastructure as part of the Enterprise tier.

enrichment.short_description: FerretDB is an open-source proxy that translates the MongoDB wire protocol into SQL, letting applications run MongoDB-compatible workloads on a PostgreSQL backend without MongoDB's SSPL license.
enrichment.seo_title: FerretDB Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FerretDB review: an open-source MongoDB-compatible database built on PostgreSQL. Explore features, licensing, FerretDB Cloud pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
FerretDB is an open-source, stateless proxy written in Go that speaks the MongoDB wire protocol on one side and translates those queries into SQL executed against a PostgreSQL-based backend on the other, so that applications, drivers, and tools built to talk to MongoDB can run against it with little or no code change while the actual data lives in an open-source relational engine rather than in MongoDB itself. The project positions itself explicitly as a direct, drop-in replacement for MongoDB 5.0 and later versions, aimed at developers and organizations who want the familiar document-database developer experience and query language that MongoDB popularized, but without being bound to MongoDB's Server Side Public License, commonly abbreviated SSPL, which MongoDB adopted in October 2018 as a replacement for its original AGPLv3 license. That licensing change proved controversial in the open-source community; the Open Source Initiative formally declined to recognize the SSPL as an approved open-source license in 2021, and major Linux distributions including Red Hat and Debian subsequently dropped MongoDB from their official package repositories over the resulting compliance concerns. FerretDB's founders cite this licensing dispute directly as the reason the project exists: they wanted a database that offered MongoDB's document-model query interface but remained genuinely, unambiguously open source.

A major architectural milestone arrived with FerretDB version 2.0, which reached general availability in March 2025 following roughly a year of collaboration with Microsoft. In this new architecture, FerretDB adopted DocumentDB, an open-source PostgreSQL extension that Microsoft originally built as the storage engine underlying Azure Cosmos DB for MongoDB's vCore offering, as its primary storage layer. FerretDB's own materials claim this shift delivers performance improvements of up to twenty times over the prior architecture, alongside new capabilities including vector search support and replication. Notably, this new version dropped SQLite as a supported backend, an option that existed in the earlier 1.x release line; the 1.x branch of the project remains available on GitHub for users who still depend on the SQLite backend, but active development and new features are concentrated on the PostgreSQL-and-DocumentDB-extension architecture going forward.

FerretDB was founded in 2021 by three people with deep roots in the open-source database world, all previously associated with Percona, the well-known open-source database services company: Peter Farkas, who serves as CEO, Alexey Palazhchenko, who serves as CTO, and Peter Zaitsev, a co-founder of Percona itself, who joined as a FerretDB co-founder as well. This pedigree is notable because it means the founding team brought direct, hands-on experience running an open-source database business at scale before starting FerretDB, likely informing decisions such as choosing PostgreSQL, one of the most widely trusted and permissively licensed open-source databases, as the underlying storage engine rather than building an entirely new storage system from scratch. The company's registered address appears to be in Wilmington, Delaware, based on LinkedIn and investor-database listings, though this looks more like a formal incorporation address for a US legal entity than a physical operating headquarters, and the actual team appears to work in a distributed fashion rather than from a single office.

On the funding side, Crunchbase records a single publicly disclosed seed round of approximately 2.8 million dollars, with investors including Basis Set Ventures and Operator Collective, though no primary press release from outlets such as TechCrunch or VentureBeat confirming the exact date and amount was located, so this figure should be treated as reasonably but not perfectly confirmed. LinkedIn's company page lists FerretDB's headcount in the two-to-ten employee range, a small team size consistent with an early-stage, engineering-heavy open-source infrastructure company, though this may understate actual headcount given the level of product activity, including the 2025 launch of a new commercial cloud offering, that the company has sustained.

The core FerretDB software itself is completely free and open source, released under the Apache 2.0 license, and there is no paywall or licensing fee to download, deploy, and run it, whether via Docker, Docker Compose, prebuilt Linux binaries, or by embedding it directly as a Go package inside another application. As of the most recent check, FerretDB's website does not have a dedicated pricing page for the core open-source product, since there is nothing to charge for at that layer; instead, the company monetizes through FerretDB Cloud, a managed database-as-a-service offering that launched on August 25, 2025. FerretDB Cloud is structured into a Free tier offering multi-tenant infrastructure with roughly ten gigabytes of storage and a 99.90 percent service level agreement, a Pro tier offering dedicated tenancy scaling up to sixty-four terabytes with a higher 99.99 percent SLA and priority support, an Enterprise tier supporting multi-cloud deployment, VPC peering, and SOC 2 audit readiness, and a separate 'Bring Your Own Account' enterprise option for organizations that want FerretDB Cloud's management layer running inside their own cloud account. No specific dollar pricing has been published for any of these paid FerretDB Cloud tiers; access currently requires joining a waitlist, and the service is presently available only on AWS, with support for Azure and Google Cloud described as planned for the future. Separately from the cloud product, FerretDB also offers enterprise support contracts, custom development, and training services, all priced on a contact-sales basis rather than published rate cards.

FerretDB's target audience consists primarily of developers and engineering organizations who want to avoid MongoDB's licensing terms and potential vendor lock-in while retaining compatibility with the large ecosystem of MongoDB drivers, tools, and developer familiarity built up over more than a decade of MongoDB's dominance in the document-database space. Rather than asking teams to learn an entirely new query language or rewrite their data access layer, FerretDB lets them keep using standard MongoDB client libraries and query syntax while their actual data is stored and managed by PostgreSQL underneath, which also opens the door to leveraging PostgreSQL's broader ecosystem of extensions, tooling, and operational maturity.

Within the competitive landscape, FerretDB positions itself most directly against MongoDB itself, particularly for organizations uncomfortable with the SSPL license, as well as against other MongoDB-protocol-compatible services such as Amazon DocumentDB and Azure Cosmos DB for MongoDB, both of which offer MongoDB API compatibility as managed cloud services from major hyperscalers rather than as an independently deployable open-source project. Percona, the company where FerretDB's founders previously worked, along with Tigris and YugabyteDB, appear in FerretDB's ecosystem as backend or infrastructure collaborators rather than as head-to-head competitors. On GitHub, the FerretDB repository has accumulated a meaningful open-source following, with just over eleven thousand stars and several hundred forks, and the project shows an active release cadence, having reached its version 1.0 general availability milestone in April 2023 and continuing to ship regular point releases through late 2025, evidence of a genuinely maintained and evolving codebase rather than an abandoned experiment, which matters considerably for any team considering it as infrastructure for production workloads.

From a market-position standpoint, FerretDB's timing tracked closely with a broader wave of database projects launched specifically in response to MongoDB's licensing shift, alongside comparable open-source-licensing responses in other categories such as Elasticsearch forks and Redis forks, reflecting a recurring pattern where a widely used open-source project's move to a more restrictive license creates room for a genuinely open alternative to attract developers who prioritize license permissiveness. FerretDB's specific bet, building compatibility on top of PostgreSQL rather than inventing a new storage engine from scratch, has proven to be a durable architectural choice, since it lets the project benefit from decades of PostgreSQL reliability engineering, its large extension ecosystem, and its widespread familiarity among database administrators, rather than asking adopters to trust an entirely new and unproven storage layer. The product's evolution from the 1.x SQLite-and-PostgreSQL-dual-backend era into the 2.0 architecture built specifically on Microsoft's DocumentDB PostgreSQL extension marks a significant strategic narrowing of scope, trading backend flexibility for substantially better performance and new capabilities like vector search, a tradeoff that suggests the FerretDB team concluded that being an excellent MongoDB-compatible layer on a best-in-class PostgreSQL-based storage engine mattered more to its target users than preserving support for lighter-weight, less scalable SQLite deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FerretDB is an open-source proxy that speaks the MongoDB wire protocol and translates queries into SQL run against a PostgreSQL-based backend, giving developers a MongoDB-compatible document database experience without MongoDB's Server Side Public License.

Founded in 2021 by former Percona team members Peter Farkas, Alexey Palazhchenko, and Peter Zaitsev, FerretDB is released under the Apache 2.0 license and, since version 2.0 in March 2025, runs on Microsoft's open-source DocumentDB PostgreSQL extension for significantly improved performance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: FerretDB acts as a drop-in replacement for MongoDB 5.0 and later, letting existing MongoDB drivers and tools connect without code changes while data is stored in PostgreSQL, and it can be deployed via Docker, Docker Compose, prebuilt Linux binaries, or embedded as a Go package.

Version 2.0 introduced Microsoft's DocumentDB PostgreSQL extension as the storage layer, claimed to deliver up to twenty times faster performance than the prior architecture along with new vector search and replication capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core FerretDB software is completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no cost to download, self-host, or embed it in an application.

FerretDB Cloud, a managed database-as-a-service launched in August 2025, offers Free, Pro, Enterprise, and Bring Your Own Account tiers, though specific dollar pricing for the paid tiers has not been published and access currently requires a waitlist on AWS.

faqs.question: Is FerretDB free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core FerretDB software is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license; a separate paid managed service called FerretDB Cloud also exists but its dollar pricing has not been publicly disclosed.

faqs.question: What database does FerretDB use for storage?
faqs.answer: FerretDB stores data in PostgreSQL, and since version 2.0 it uses Microsoft's open-source DocumentDB PostgreSQL extension for improved performance and features like vector search and replication.

faqs.question: Why was FerretDB created?
faqs.answer: FerretDB was created in response to MongoDB's move to the Server Side Public License in 2018, which the Open Source Initiative does not recognize as approved open source, giving developers a genuinely open-source, MongoDB-compatible alternative.

faqs.question: Can I replace MongoDB with FerretDB without changing my application code?
faqs.answer: FerretDB is designed as a drop-in replacement for MongoDB 5.0 and later, translating the MongoDB wire protocol into SQL, so most existing MongoDB drivers and tools can connect with little to no code change.

faqs.question: Who founded FerretDB?
faqs.answer: FerretDB was founded in 2021 by Peter Farkas, Alexey Palazhchenko, and Peter Zaitsev, all previously associated with the open-source database company Percona.

faqs.question: Does FerretDB support SQLite?
faqs.answer: SQLite was supported as a backend in the FerretDB 1.x release line, but it was dropped in version 2.0, which moved to PostgreSQL with the DocumentDB extension; the 1.x branch remains available on GitHub for legacy SQLite users.

enrichment.short_description: Fetcher is an AI-powered recruiting platform that combines automated candidate sourcing across a large talent database with human sourcer review and automated outreach sequencing.
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Fetcher Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fetcher review: AI-driven candidate sourcing and outreach automation for recruiting teams. Compare pricing tiers, key features, and alternatives like SeekOut.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fetcher is a recruiting technology platform, operated under the legal entity name Scout Technologies, that positions itself as a sourcing 'autopilot' for talent acquisition teams. Its core function is to combine an AI-driven search layer over a candidate database reported to exceed five hundred million profiles with a human review step performed by Fetcher's own sourcing specialists, so that instead of a recruiter manually running boolean search strings across LinkedIn and other channels, Fetcher's system surfaces both passive and active candidates matched to a role and then automates the outreach process through templated, sequenced email campaigns designed to engage those candidates. This hybrid model, blending machine search with human curation before candidates ever reach a hiring team's inbox, is central to how Fetcher differentiates itself from purely self-serve, do-it-yourself sourcing tools that expect the recruiter to do all of the searching and vetting personally.

The company's origins trace back to a 2014 launch as a professional networking application called Caliber, which subsequently pivoted into the recruitment automation product now known as Fetcher, with most sources treating 2015 as the founding year of the recruiting-focused business, though the exact year varies slightly depending on the source and should be treated as approximate rather than definitively pinned to a single date. Fetcher's founders are Andres Blank, Christopher Calmeyn, Javier Castiarena, Santiago Aimetta, and Genevieve Wolff Jurvetson, a five-person founding team, and the company is headquartered in New York City. Fetcher raised a seed round backed by Slow Ventures and Accomplice, though the exact dollar amount of that round is not publicly disclosed. It subsequently raised a 6.5 million dollar Series A that closed in March 2021, followed by a 27 million dollar Series B announced in May 2022 that was led by Tola Capital with participation from G20 Ventures, KFund, and Accomplice, bringing total disclosed funding to approximately 40 million dollars as reported at the time of the Series B announcement. Employee headcount estimates vary meaningfully across third-party data sources, with LinkedIn placing the company in the fifty-one to two hundred employee range while other trackers report figures both somewhat higher and somewhat lower, so any specific current headcount figure should be treated as an approximation.

Fetcher's pricing, published on its own pricing page, is structured into several named tiers rather than a single flat subscription. The entry-level Self-serve tier costs 115 dollars per month and includes three hundred leads per month, one user seat, and access to the full candidate database of more than five hundred million profiles, aimed at individual recruiters who want to do their own sourcing with AI assistance but without dedicated human support. The Growth tier costs 379 dollars per month and expands capacity to up to twenty-five hundred leads per year along with one seat and basic analytics, described by the company as a do-it-yourself oriented plan for teams that want more volume than the entry tier but are not yet ready for full-service sourcing support. The Amplify tier, at 649 dollars per month, is the full-service offering, providing a dedicated human sourcer covering four to six open roles simultaneously, two user seats, and access to Fetcher's broader customer success team, effectively combining the AI sourcing engine with hands-on human recruiting support. Above these self-serve tiers sits a custom, volume-priced Enterprise tier for larger organizations with more complex needs. All tiers include core capabilities such as email integration, templated outreach sequences, a LinkedIn sourcing browser plugin, and applicant tracking system integration, and Fetcher offers a thirty percent discount for customers who choose annual billing over monthly billing.

On the integration side, Fetcher connects to more than twenty applicant tracking systems through Merge's unified API layer, including widely used platforms such as Greenhouse, Lever, SmartRecruiters, BambooHR, Jobvite, iCIMS, JazzHR, Breezy, Recruiterflow, Recruitee, and Workable, along with a Slack integration for team notifications, which allows recruiting teams to keep Fetcher's sourced candidates flowing directly into their existing hiring workflow rather than operating as a disconnected, standalone tool. Fetcher's own marketing materials cite performance statistics including an average candidate vetting time of twenty-three seconds, seventeen hours saved per role filled, an average email response rate around forty percent, and roughly twenty thousand dollars in annual savings per recruiter, though these figures come from the company's own reporting rather than independently audited studies and should be understood as vendor-provided metrics.

Fetcher's target customer base is recruiting and talent acquisition teams at growth-stage companies, spanning sourcing needs across functions including engineering, finance, product, operations, marketing, sales, and customer success roles, rather than being narrowly specialized in any single job category. Named customers referenced in Fetcher's own case studies and press materials include Magnite, Counterpart, Temedica, Docker, Deutsche Telekom, Shutterstock, Behr Paint, Albertsons, and Foursquare, spanning both technology companies and larger, more traditional consumer and enterprise organizations. Customer count figures reported over time range from 'over 350 organizations' at the time of the 2022 Series B announcement to a more recent, less rigorously sourced figure of 'over 1,000 organizations across four continents,' a growth trajectory consistent with the company's continued fundraising and product expansion, though the more recent figure comes from a less authoritative source and should be treated with some caution.

Within the competitive recruiting technology landscape, Fetcher is most commonly compared against SeekOut, known for deep technical and engineering-focused candidate indexing; HireEZ, which markets itself around an AI-driven replacement for manual boolean search with a database exceeding eight hundred million profiles; Gem, which focuses heavily on pipeline management and outreach automation for recruiting teams; and Loxo, an all-in-one platform combining CRM, applicant tracking, and sourcing in a single system. Fetcher differentiates itself within this set primarily through its hybrid AI-plus-human-sourcer model, particularly evident in its higher-tier Amplify plan, and through the reported strength of its email outreach response rates, positioning it as less of a pure self-serve search tool and more of an outsourced sourcing function delivered through software, which appeals particularly to lean in-house recruiting teams that lack the bandwidth to run high-volume sourcing campaigns entirely on their own.

On the technical and operational side, Fetcher's platform is built around continuously refreshed access to its underlying candidate database, meaning the five-hundred-million-plus profile figure the company cites is presented as a living dataset rather than a static snapshot, kept current through ongoing data collection and enrichment processes that are not published in granular technical detail but that the company's marketing materials tie directly to the accuracy of its AI matching layer. The platform's architecture separates the AI-driven candidate discovery and ranking step from the outreach automation step, allowing recruiting teams on lower tiers to use the sourcing engine independently of the higher-touch, human-assisted workflow reserved for the Amplify tier, a segmentation that mirrors how Fetcher has structured its pricing around gradually increasing service intensity rather than purely around feature unlocks. In terms of market position, Fetcher sits within a recruiting technology category that expanded significantly during the 2020s as talent acquisition teams sought to automate increasingly labor-intensive sourcing work, competing against both pure AI-search tools and more full-service, higher-touch recruiting process outsourcing providers; Fetcher's hybrid approach, blending automated matching with human sourcer oversight, positions it somewhere between these two poles, which the company has leaned into as its primary differentiator rather than competing purely on the size of its candidate database or the sophistication of its matching algorithm alone. Since its 2022 Series B, Fetcher's product evolution has continued to emphasize deeper ATS integration coverage and expanded analytics, reflecting a broader industry trend toward recruiting tools that plug directly into a company's existing hiring stack rather than operating as a standalone destination that recruiters must check separately.

enrichment.short_description: FHEM is a Perl-based, community-maintained open-source home automation server that controls and automates smart home devices across a wide range of protocols including Homematic, KNX, ZigBee, and MQTT.
enrichment.seo_title: FHEM Review 2026: Features, Setup and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FHEM review: the Perl-based open-source home automation server. Explore its module ecosystem, licensing, self-hosting setup, and alternatives like Home Assistant.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
FHEM is an open-source home automation server, written entirely in Perl, that acts as the central brain for controlling and automating smart home devices in a residence. Its name is an acronym for the German phrase 'Freundliche Hausautomation und Energie-Messung,' which translates roughly to 'Friendly Home Automation and Energy Measurement,' reflecting both its country of origin and its original emphasis on energy monitoring alongside device control. The software follows a modular, plugin-style architecture with more than four hundred thirty distinct modules, each responsible for supporting a specific device type or communication protocol, allowing FHEM to interface with an unusually broad range of smart home hardware standards including Homematic, MAX! thermostats, older FS20 and FHT radio-frequency protocols, KNX, Z-Wave, ZigBee, EnOcean, 1-Wire sensors, MQTT, Wireless M-Bus, RFXCOM receivers, Philips Hue, AVM's FRITZ!DECT smart plugs, and integrations with Apple HomeKit and Amazon Alexa, as well as direct control of numerous audio-video equipment brands such as LG, Sonos, and Denon. Users interact with FHEM through a built-in lightweight web server component called FHEMWEB, which serves a browser-based control interface by default on port 8083 with optional HTTPS and basic authentication, though the system can also be controlled through dedicated smartphone apps, a telnet interface, or direct TCP/IP commands for more advanced integrations and scripting.

FHEM was created by a German developer named Rudolf König, based in Frankfurt, who began building the software in 2005 to solve a specific personal problem: controlling FHT-brand radio-controlled heating thermostats in his own house. What started as a narrowly scoped personal utility for heating control gradually expanded, through König's continued development and a growing community of contributors, into a general-purpose home automation platform capable of managing lighting, security sensors, energy metering, entertainment systems, and virtually any other networked or radio-controlled device with an available FHEM module. This history places FHEM's origins several years before the two home automation platforms it is most commonly compared against today: openHAB, which emerged around 2010, and Home Assistant, which launched in 2013, making FHEM the oldest of the three major open-source home automation platforms still in active use, a distinction that shapes both its strengths, such as unusually deep and mature support for older German-market radio-frequency protocols like FS20 and 433 megahertz devices, and some of its perceived weaknesses, such as a design that predates more modern integration patterns and that some users describe as more resource-intensive than newer alternatives.

Unlike most software platforms of comparable scope, FHEM has never been the product of a company. It is, and has always been, a purely community-driven open-source project released under the GNU General Public License version 2. Its governance and infrastructure are supported by FHEM e.V., a registered German nonprofit association, or eingetragener Verein, that was founded on December 13, 2015, and officially registered on July 14, 2016, in Frankfurt am Main, the same city where the project originated. FHEM e.V. maintains the community's forum, wiki, and related infrastructure, formally taking over hosting of the community forum at forum.fhem.de onto its own servers in February 2017. The project's actual source code is version-controlled using Subversion, hosted at svn.fhem.de, with the project's own contributor tracking indicating roughly one hundred fifty registered active contributors, though this figure comes from the project's own Trac instance and has not been independently verified. A read-only mirror of the codebase is also published to GitHub at github.com/fhem/fhem-mirror, but the project explicitly states that pull requests submitted to that mirror's master branch will be rejected, underscoring that Subversion, not Git or GitHub, remains the actual, authoritative development workflow for the project, a somewhat unusual choice by 2026 standards that reflects the project's long, continuous history predating the more recent dominance of Git-based open-source workflows.

Because FHEM has no commercial entity behind it, there is no paid tier, no hosted cloud version, and no subscription of any kind; the software is entirely free to download and use, and it must be self-hosted by the user on their own hardware. In practice this typically means running FHEM on a Raspberry Pi, a network-attached storage device, a general-purpose Linux server, or in some cases directly on a router such as an AVM FRITZ!Box, since FHEM's Perl-based runtime is lightweight enough to operate on modest embedded hardware. The platform is also compatible with Windows, through the Cygwin compatibility layer, as well as macOS, general Unix systems, OpenBSD, and FreeBSD, giving it broad flexibility in deployment environment, though it fundamentally requires a working Perl interpreter, and controlling USB-connected radio devices additionally requires the Device::SerialPort Perl module. No official minimum RAM or CPU specifications have been published by the project, though its long history of running well on constrained hardware like early Raspberry Pi models suggests fairly modest resource requirements by modern standards.

For automation logic, FHEM offers two main approaches that together define much of its power and flexibility for advanced users. The simpler built-in mechanism is called notify, which triggers predefined commands in response to specific events or scheduled times, suitable for straightforward rules such as turning on a light at sunset. For more sophisticated conditional logic, the community-developed DOIF module, whose name stands for 'DO IF,' provides a more declarative, readable syntax for expressing complex if-elseif-else style automation rules directly within FHEM's configuration, without requiring users to write full Perl scripts for every piece of conditional behavior, though full custom Perl scripting remains available for users who need capabilities beyond what notify or DOIF can express.

FHEM's target audience is technically inclined do-it-yourself home automation hobbyists who are comfortable running a server continuously in their home and, often, purchasing and configuring specialized radio hardware such as CUL USB sticks, EnOcean transceivers, or Z-Wave controllers to bridge FHEM to their physical devices. The project's community is strongly oriented toward German-speaking users; its primary community forum, forum.fhem.de, conducts the overwhelming majority of its discussion in German, though English-language participation is explicitly welcomed, and the supporting FHEM e.V. nonprofit organization is itself a German-registered entity. There is, notably, no English-language Wikipedia article about FHEM, only a German-language entry, with a separate Italian-language entry also existing according to Wikidata, further underscoring the project's primarily German and, to a lesser extent, broader European user base rather than a significant English-speaking or North American following.

Within the competitive landscape of open-source home automation platforms, FHEM is most frequently compared against Home Assistant, openHAB, ioBroker, Domoticz, and Pimatic, all of which appear together repeatedly in cross-community forum discussions where users debate migrating between platforms. As the oldest of these major platforms, FHEM carries particular legacy strength in supporting older, largely German-market radio-frequency device protocols that some newer platforms support less comprehensively, but some long-time users have cited its comparatively higher resource consumption and its older, less modern architecture as reasons they have migrated toward newer alternatives like Home Assistant, which has grown rapidly in international popularity and now maintains a substantially larger English-language user base and integration ecosystem. Despite this competitive pressure, FHEM remains actively maintained, with frequent commits visible in its Subversion repository and a most recent major version, 6.4, released March 29, 2025, indicating that the project, now more than two decades old, continues to receive genuine ongoing development from its community rather than existing purely as legacy software kept alive out of inertia.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FHEM is a Perl-based, self-hosted home automation server created in 2005 by German developer Rudolf König, originally to control radio-based heating thermostats and later expanded into a general-purpose smart home platform supporting more than four hundred thirty device and protocol modules.

The project has no commercial owner and is governed as a community effort supported by FHEM e.V., a registered German nonprofit association, with its source code maintained on Subversion and released under the GNU General Public License version 2.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: FHEM's modular architecture supports an unusually wide range of smart home protocols, including Homematic, KNX, Z-Wave, ZigBee, EnOcean, MQTT, 1-Wire, Philips Hue, and FRITZ!DECT, alongside integrations with Apple HomeKit and Amazon Alexa.

Automation logic can be built using the simple built-in notify mechanism for basic triggers, or the more powerful community-developed DOIF module for declarative, conditional automation rules, with full Perl scripting available for advanced use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: FHEM is entirely free and open source under the GPLv2 license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or hosted cloud version offered by the project.

Because there is no commercial entity behind FHEM, users must self-host it themselves, typically on a Raspberry Pi, NAS device, Linux server, or router, running on Linux, Windows via Cygwin, macOS, or BSD systems.

features.title: Active community maintenance
features.description: Supported by FHEM e.V., a registered German nonprofit, with an active Subversion repository and regular version releases including version 6.4 in March 2025.

pros: Completely free and open source under the GPLv2 license with no commercial restrictions
Unusually broad support for legacy and niche radio-frequency protocols, especially in the German market
Mature, two-decade-old codebase with an active community and regular releases
Highly flexible automation logic via notify and the DOIF module, plus full Perl scripting for advanced users
cons: Documentation and community discussion are predominantly in German, which can be a barrier for English-only users
Older architecture that some long-time users describe as more resource-intensive than newer platforms
Requires real technical comfort with Perl, Subversion-based updates, and radio hardware for full setup
No official mobile-first design or modern app-store apps comparable to newer competitors

faqs.question: Is FHEM free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, FHEM is entirely free and open source under the GNU General Public License version 2, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or hosted cloud version.

faqs.question: Who created FHEM?
faqs.answer: FHEM was created in 2005 by German developer Rudolf König, originally to control radio-based heating thermostats in his own house, and has since grown into a community-maintained project.

faqs.question: Is FHEM a company?
faqs.answer: No, FHEM has never been a company product; it is supported by FHEM e.V., a registered German nonprofit association founded in 2015 that maintains the project's community forum and infrastructure.

faqs.question: What devices and protocols does FHEM support?
faqs.answer: FHEM supports more than four hundred thirty modules covering protocols including Homematic, KNX, Z-Wave, ZigBee, EnOcean, MQTT, 1-Wire, and integrations with Apple HomeKit and Amazon Alexa.

faqs.question: How is FHEM different from Home Assistant or openHAB?
faqs.answer: FHEM predates both Home Assistant and openHAB, having launched in 2005, and has particularly deep support for older German-market radio-frequency protocols, though some users find its architecture more resource-intensive than these newer alternatives.

faqs.question: What do I need to run FHEM?
faqs.answer: FHEM requires a Perl interpreter and is commonly self-hosted on a Raspberry Pi, NAS device, Linux server, or router, running on Linux, Windows via Cygwin, macOS, or BSD; controlling USB radio devices additionally requires the Device::SerialPort Perl module.

use_cases.title: Controlling legacy German smart home hardware
use_cases.description: Hobbyists with older FS20, FHT, or Homematic radio-frequency devices use FHEM for its unusually mature and comprehensive support of these protocols.

use_cases.title: Building a fully self-hosted smart home
use_cases.description: Privacy-focused users deploy FHEM on a Raspberry Pi or home server to control lighting, heating, and sensors without relying on any cloud service.

use_cases.title: Complex conditional home automation rules
use_cases.description: Advanced users write DOIF rules or custom Perl scripts within FHEM to build sophisticated, multi-condition automation logic across many integrated devices.
use_cases.audience: Technical power users and automation tinkerers

enrichment.short_description: Fibery is a flexible, no-code work management platform where teams build custom entity types, relations, whiteboards, and reports to create their own tailored project and knowledge management system.
enrichment.seo_title: Fibery Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fibery review: a flexible no-code work management platform combining custom databases, docs, whiteboards, and automations. Compare pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fibery is a work management platform built around an unusually flexible, no-code data model that lets teams define their own custom entity types, such as features, bugs, customers, or any other object relevant to their work, and connect those entity types together through relations, rather than forcing teams into a fixed, predefined structure of boards, lists, or tickets the way many mainstream project management tools do. On top of this relational foundation, Fibery layers multiple ways to view and interact with the same underlying data, including Table, Board, Gallery, List, Timeline, Gantt, Calendar, Map, Report, and Dashboard views, alongside collaborative rich-text documents, real-time whiteboards, formulas for computed fields, and automations or rules that can trigger actions based on changes in the data. The company describes its own ambition as building an 'operating system for organizations,' explicitly aimed at consolidating the several separate tools, typically a project tracker, a documentation wiki, and a whiteboarding app, that many teams currently stitch together, into a single coherent workspace built on one shared, interconnected data model.

Fibery's origins trace back to 2017, when it began as an internal research and development project inside Targetprocess, an agile project management tool built by the same two founders, Michael Dubakov and Vadim Gaidukevich. Targetprocess was eventually acquired by Apptio, which was itself later acquired by IBM, and Fibery became a fully independent company in its own right around 2021, following the completion of the Targetprocess acquisition, after having run a private beta starting in 2019. This lineage matters because it means Fibery's founders had already spent years building and refining a flexible, data-model-driven project management tool at Targetprocess before starting fresh with Fibery, likely informing the even more radically flexible, user-definable entity system that became Fibery's core differentiator. The company operates as a remote-first team, with stated offices in Warsaw, Poland, and Nicosia, Cyprus, rather than a single centralized headquarters in the traditional sense.

On funding, Fibery has raised a total of approximately 8.3 million dollars across two publicly disclosed rounds: a 3.1 million dollar seed round around August 2021 led by AltaIR Capital, and a 5.2 million dollar Series A around August 2022 led by Tal Ventures with continued participation from AltaIR Capital. These are comparatively modest amounts by venture-funded SaaS standards, and third-party revenue trackers have reported Fibery's 2024 annual recurring revenue at approximately 1.8 million dollars, up from roughly 661,000 dollars in 2023, framing the company's growth narrative as substantially bootstrapped and organically driven rather than fueled primarily by large venture capital injections. Company size estimates converge around thirty to thirty-six employees based on the company's own 'about us' page, which self-reports approximately thirty people, alongside similar third-party estimates from sources like Latka and PitchBook, making Fibery a genuinely small, lean team relative to the scope and technical sophistication of the product it maintains.

Fibery's pricing, published on its pricing page in euros, follows a four-tier structure. The Free tier costs nothing and supports up to ten users plus ten guest accounts, with up to ten separate databases, unlimited entities, documents, formulas, automations, and integrations, making it a genuinely usable tier for small teams rather than a token trial. The Standard tier costs twelve euros per user per month on annual billing, removes the user limit entirely, and adds unlimited databases, AI features, charts, whiteboards, and human customer support beyond the Free tier's self-serve resources. The Pro tier costs twenty euros per user per month on annual billing and adds more advanced AI capabilities, user groups, more granular permissions, JavaScript-based custom automations, and unlimited version history for tracking changes over time. The Enterprise tier costs forty euros per user per month, requires a minimum of twenty-five users, and adds unlimited automations and integrations, SAML-based single sign-on, SCIM user provisioning, regional data residency options, and a dedicated customer success manager. Every new Fibery workspace additionally receives an automatic fourteen-day trial of the Pro tier regardless of which plan the customer ultimately intends to use, and the company offers meaningful discounts for specific groups, including six months of free Pro-tier access for qualifying startups, fifty percent discounts for non-profits, educational institutions, and Ukrainian organizations, and one hundred percent discounts, meaning fully free access, for open-source projects.

Fibery's target audience skews toward technical and product-oriented teams: software and game development studios, digital agencies, and startups that want a single flexible workspace rather than juggling separate specialized tools for project tracking, documentation, and diagramming. The company's own marketing language explicitly and somewhat playfully targets 'organizations run by nerds,' signaling a self-aware positioning toward technically sophisticated users who are comfortable configuring a flexible system to their exact needs rather than wanting a rigid, opinionated, out-of-the-box tool. Beyond its core technical-team audience, Fibery also markets toward non-profit, education, consulting, construction, and real estate organizations, industries where the underlying relational-database flexibility can be repurposed for very different kinds of structured record-keeping beyond software product development.

Within the crowded work management and productivity software category, Fibery is most frequently compared, through its own published comparison pages, against Notion, Airtable, Asana, ClickUp, Linear, Jira, and Trello, a set spanning both flexible all-in-one workspace tools like Notion and Airtable and more specialized, opinionated project trackers like Linear and Jira. Fibery's core differentiation within this set is its unusually deep relational data model: while tools like Notion and Airtable also offer some degree of custom database functionality, Fibery pushes the concept further by treating relations between different entity types as a first-class, central organizing principle of the entire workspace rather than a secondary feature layered onto documents or spreadsheets, which the company argues makes it uniquely suited for teams that need to model genuinely complex, interconnected workflows, such as tracking how customer feedback connects to product features, which connect to engineering tasks, which connect to release notes, all within a single, queryable system rather than across several disconnected tools.

Beyond the core relational database and view system, Fibery has continued to expand its feature set to include more than twenty-five native integrations with other business tools, along with support for connecting to broader automation platforms such as Zapier, Make, and n8n, plus a documented API for custom integrations, and increasingly, built-in AI capabilities including what the company calls a Smart Agent and semantic search across the workspace's content. While specific named enterprise customer logos were not found in publicly available materials at the time of research, third-party estimates place Fibery's customer base at approximately 2,500 organizations as of 2024, though this figure comes from a secondary source rather than being confirmed directly by Fibery, and should be treated as an approximate, unverified indicator of the company's market traction rather than an authoritative count.

On the technical architecture side, Fibery's relational database engine is the layer that makes its many views possible: because every Table, Board, Timeline, Gantt, or Calendar view is simply a different visual projection of the same underlying entities and relations, changes made in one view propagate instantly to every other view of that data, and users can filter, group, and sort any view using the full relational structure rather than being limited to properties defined on a single flat record. This same relational core underlies Fibery's automation and formula systems, since rules and computed fields can reference related entities across the graph of connections a team has built, allowing genuinely cross-functional automations, for example automatically updating a customer record's status based on the state of several linked support tickets, that would require much more manual wiring in a flatter, less relationally aware tool. In terms of market position, Fibery competes in a crowded and well-funded work management category dominated by much larger, more heavily marketed players such as Notion, Airtable, and Asana, and its comparatively modest funding and lean team size mean it has grown primarily through product-led, word-of-mouth adoption among technically sophisticated teams rather than through large-scale enterprise sales or marketing spend. This positioning has shaped Fibery's product evolution: rather than chasing the broadest possible mass-market audience, the company has continued to deepen its relational data model, AI search, and automation capabilities aimed specifically at technical and product teams willing to invest time in configuring a system to their exact workflow, a strategy that trades a larger total addressable market for stronger retention and expansion within its chosen niche.

paragraphs: Fibery is a flexible, no-code work management platform where teams define their own custom entity types and relations, then view and manage that data through Table, Board, Timeline, Gantt, Calendar, and other views, alongside docs, whiteboards, and automations, all in one connected workspace.

Started in 2017 as an internal project inside Targetprocess by founders Michael Dubakov and Vadim Gaidukevich, Fibery became a fully independent, remote-first company around 2021 and has raised approximately 8.3 million dollars while growing largely through bootstrapped, organic revenue.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Fibery's core differentiator is its relational database model, where custom entity types like features, bugs, or customers can be linked through relations and viewed through more than nine different view types, all reflecting the same underlying data.

The platform also includes collaborative documents, real-time whiteboards, formula-based computed fields, automations and rules, embedded reporting and dashboards, more than twenty-five native integrations, and AI features including semantic search and a Smart Agent.

Every new workspace gets a fourteen-day trial of the Pro tier, and Fibery offers discounts for startups, non-profits, education, Ukrainian organizations, and free access for open-source projects.
