enrichment.short_description: Figma is a browser-based, real-time collaborative interface design platform used by product teams for UI design, prototyping, whiteboarding, and developer handoff, now publicly traded on the NYSE.
enrichment.seo_title: Figma Review 2026: Features, Pricing, IPO and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Figma review: the browser-based collaborative design platform behind the blocked Adobe deal and 2025 IPO. Explore features, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Figma is a browser-based, real-time collaborative interface design platform that fundamentally changed how product design teams work by making design files feel more like Google Docs than traditional desktop software: multiple people can open the same file simultaneously, see each other's cursors and edits live, and collaborate without exporting, emailing, or manually merging files. At its technical core, Figma's rendering engine is written in C++ and compiled to run inside the browser using WebAssembly, via the Emscripten toolchain, a genuinely unusual architectural choice at the time of the company's early development, since it meant building what amounted to a professional-grade, native-quality vector graphics application that ran entirely inside a web page rather than as an installed desktop program. When Figma first built this engine, WebGL, an API originally designed for 3D graphics in the browser, was chosen as the rendering foundation, itself an atypical decision for what is fundamentally a 2D design tool. As browser technology matured, Figma migrated its renderer from WebGL to WebGPU following Chromium's rollout of WebGPU support in 2023, a move that unlocked compute shaders, letting more rendering work run on the GPU instead of the CPU, adopted the WGSL shading language, and added multisample anti-aliasing support. The renderer itself handles vector curve rendering using the Loop-Blinn algorithm, along with image handling, blur effects, layer masking and blending, and dithered gradients, all with anti-aliasing applied directly on the GPU. Real-time multiplayer collaboration, arguably Figma's single most defining technical feature, runs through a set of Rust-based multiplayer servers communicating over WebSockets; when one user edits a file, the resulting edit deltas are transmitted to other connected clients and applied directly inside the browser's WebAssembly memory heap against an in-memory scene graph, meaning the collaborative editing logic never has to round-trip through the browser's Document Object Model, which is central to how Figma achieves the low-latency, near-instantaneous feel of its multiplayer editing experience even with several people working in the same file at once.

Figma was founded in 2012 by Dylan Field and Evan Wallace, who met while studying computer science together at Brown University. Field's path to founding the company was shaped significantly by winning a Thiel Fellowship in May 2012, a program created by PayPal co-founder Peter Thiel that awards young entrepreneurs one hundred thousand dollars on the condition that they leave traditional schooling for two years to build a company; Field accepted the fellowship, dropped out of Brown, and relocated with Wallace to San Francisco to begin building what would become Figma. The company then spent roughly four years in a relatively quiet, heads-down building phase, a long gestation period by modern startup standards, reflecting the genuine technical difficulty of building a professional-grade, real-time collaborative design tool from scratch inside a web browser. Figma's first public-facing product milestone came with a free, invite-only preview launched on December 3, 2015, and the company's first full, generally available public release followed on September 27, 2016, nearly four years after the company's founding, a launch that introduced the broader design community to browser-based collaborative design at a moment when the dominant tools in the category, Adobe's applications and Sketch, were still fundamentally native desktop programs without real-time multiplayer editing.

Over the following decade, Figma raised substantial venture capital across a well-documented sequence of funding rounds: a seed round of roughly 3.8 to 4 million dollars in June 2013 led by Index Ventures at an approximate valuation of 16 million dollars; a 14 million dollar Series A in December 2015 led by Greylock Partners at roughly a 77 million dollar valuation, timed close to the company's initial preview launch; a 25 million dollar Series B in February 2018 led by Kleiner Perkins at approximately a 159 million dollar valuation; a 40 million dollar Series C in February 2019 led by Sequoia Capital at around a 440 million dollar valuation; a 50 million dollar Series D in April 2020 led by Andreessen Horowitz, which pushed the company past unicorn status at an approximate 2 billion dollar valuation; and a 200 million dollar Series E in June 2021 led by Durable Capital Partners at a valuation of roughly 10 billion dollars. In total, Figma raised approximately 749 million dollars in venture funding prior to going public, though exact aggregate figures vary slightly depending on the tracking source. A secondary-market tender offer in 2024, involving investors including Fidelity, valued the company at approximately 12.5 billion dollars after a pivotal event earlier that year, while other trackers placed Figma's immediate post-event valuation somewhat lower, in the 8.3 to 9 billion dollar range; these figures represent informal secondary-market marks rather than formal primary funding rounds and should be read as approximate.

The pivotal event referenced above was Adobe's attempt to acquire Figma outright. On September 15, 2022, Adobe signed a definitive agreement to acquire Figma for approximately 20 billion dollars, structured as roughly half cash and half Adobe stock, a deal that would have been Adobe's largest acquisition in its history and valued Figma at roughly double its prior private-market valuation. The proposed acquisition immediately drew intense antitrust scrutiny from regulators in both the European Union and the United Kingdom, who raised concerns that combining Adobe, whose product portfolio already included the design tool Adobe XD, with Figma, the clear market leader in collaborative interface design, would eliminate meaningful competition between the two most significant players in product design software. After more than a year of regulatory review and mounting opposition, Adobe and Figma jointly announced on December 18, 2023, that they had mutually agreed to terminate the merger agreement, stating in their joint announcement and in Adobe's corresponding SEC filing that there was no clear path to receiving the necessary regulatory approvals from the European Commission and the UK's Competition and Markets Authority. Under the terms of the original merger agreement's reverse termination clause, Adobe paid Figma a termination fee of one billion dollars, which Figma received on December 20, 2023, a substantial sum that both compensated Figma for the deal's collapse and provided the company with significant additional capital heading into its next phase as an independent company. In the aftermath, Adobe notably de-emphasized and largely discontinued active development of its own competing product, Adobe XD.

That next phase culminated in Figma's initial public offering. The company filed its S-1 registration statement around July 1, 2025, and began trading publicly on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol FIG on July 31, 2025. Figma priced its IPO at 33 dollars per share, covering just under 37 million Class A shares, a mix of newly issued shares from the company and shares sold by existing stockholders, pricing above its initially expected range and implying an offering valuation of roughly 19.3 billion dollars. The stock's public debut was dramatic even by the standards of high-profile technology IPOs: shares more than tripled on the first day of trading, briefly triggering a trading halt after crossing 112 dollars, and ultimately closed the day at 115.50 dollars, representing an approximate 250 percent gain from the IPO price and pushing Figma's market capitalization to roughly 68 billion dollars by the close of its first trading day. By mid-2026, however, available financial tracking indicated the stock had fallen substantially from those first-day highs, trading below 20 dollars with a market capitalization under 10 billion dollars, alongside reported fiscal year 2025 figures of approximately 1.06 billion dollars in revenue against a net or operating loss in the range of 1.25 to 1.29 billion dollars, a loss figure that likely reflects large, largely non-cash stock-based compensation expenses typical of a company in its first year as a newly public entity rather than representing a comparable operating loss in the traditional sense, though this specific interpretation was not independently verified in depth.

Figma is led by Dylan Field, who serves as Chairman and CEO, and the company is headquartered in San Francisco, California. Reported employee headcount varies meaningfully by source and date, ranging from approximately 1,600 to 1,900 employees in late 2025 according to some trackers, up to as many as roughly 2,900 by one estimate in early 2026, a range that likely reflects both genuine hiring growth following the IPO and differing methodologies across the data sources reporting these figures. Figma's own reported user metrics describe approximately 13 million monthly active users, with roughly 85 percent of that usage occurring outside the United States, reflecting the company's genuinely global adoption pattern; notable customers referenced in press coverage include Microsoft, Netflix, Airbnb, Stripe, Slack, Zoom, Dropbox, Duolingo, GitHub, Uber, LinkedIn, The New York Times, and Anthropic, spanning technology, media, and enterprise organizations across essentially every major industry vertical.

Figma's current pricing structure, published on its own pricing page, spans four tiers. The Starter tier is free and includes a limited number of files, three Design or Sites files, three FigJam files, and three Slides files, along with unlimited draft files and 150 AI credits per day up to a monthly cap of 500. The Professional tier costs 16 dollars per month for a Full seat, 12 dollars per month for a Dev seat, and 3 dollars per month for a Collab seat. The Organization tier, available only with annual billing, costs 55 dollars per month for a Full seat, 25 dollars per month for a Dev seat, and 5 dollars per month for a Collab seat. The Enterprise tier, also annual-only and further customizable through direct negotiation, costs 90 dollars per month for a Full seat, 35 dollars per month for a Dev seat, and 5 dollars per month for a Collab seat. Full seats provide access across Figma's entire product suite, including Design, Make, Draw, Sites, Dev Mode, FigJam, Slides, and Buzz, while Dev and Collab seats provide more limited, role-specific access at lower price points, and dedicated Viewer seats remain free across all tiers for stakeholders who only need to review and comment on files rather than edit them.

Beyond its original core design product, Figma has expanded substantially into a broader suite of connected tools. FigJam, a real-time collaborative whiteboarding product, launched in April 2021 and extended Figma's multiplayer collaboration technology beyond interface design into open-ended brainstorming and workshop facilitation. Dev Mode, launched in June 2023, gave developers a dedicated view within Figma files optimized for inspecting design specifications, extracting code snippets, and managing the handoff between design and engineering teams. Figma Slides, a presentation-building tool, entered beta in June 2024. Figma Sites, a tool for building and publishing web pages directly from design files, launched in May 2025. Most significantly for the platform's ongoing evolution, Figma Make, an AI-powered tool that converts text prompts or existing designs into working, interactive prototypes and applications through a conversational interface, was announced at the company's Config 2025 conference on May 7, 2025, reflecting the broader industry-wide push toward AI-assisted design and development tooling. Figma further expanded its AI capabilities in October 2025 by acquiring the AI startup Weavy for a reported figure exceeding 200 million dollars, subsequently rebranding the acquired technology internally as Figma Weave.

Within the competitive landscape of design and product creation tools, Figma is most commonly compared against Adobe XD, though Adobe has largely de-emphasized that product following the collapsed acquisition; Sketch, a native macOS application that added its own browser-based collaboration features in response to Figma's rise but remains platform-limited compared to Figma's fully browser-based, cross-platform approach; InVision, which originally competed directly in prototyping before pivoting its business primarily toward its Freehand whiteboarding product after struggling to compete with Figma's integrated design-and-prototyping workflow; Canva, a broader, more mass-market graphic design tool that competes only partially with Figma given Canva's focus on marketing and social content rather than structured interface design systems; Framer, which combines interface design with the ability to publish fully functional, hosted websites directly from design files; and Penpot, an open-source, self-hostable alternative explicitly positioned for organizations wanting a Figma-like collaborative design experience without depending on a proprietary, venture-backed, now-public company. Trade press commentary generally continues to describe Figma as the default choice for design teams of three or more people as of 2026, citing its pioneering real-time multiplayer collaboration, an extensive ecosystem of more than 5,000 community-built plugins, and Dev Mode's design-to-code handoff capabilities as durable competitive advantages that have proven difficult for rivals to fully replicate even years after Figma's initial market breakthrough.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Figma is a browser-based, real-time collaborative interface design platform, founded in 2012 by Dylan Field and Evan Wallace, that pioneered multiplayer design editing inside the web browser using a custom C++ rendering engine compiled to WebAssembly.

The company survived a blocked twenty billion dollar acquisition attempt by Adobe in 2022-2023, received a one billion dollar termination fee when the deal collapsed, and went public on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker FIG in July 2025.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Figma's core design product includes vector editing, reusable components, auto layout, variables, and prototyping, alongside Dev Mode for developer handoff, all built on a real-time multiplayer collaboration engine that syncs edits instantly across every connected user.

The broader Figma suite has expanded to include FigJam for whiteboarding, Figma Slides for presentations, Figma Sites for publishing web pages, and Figma Make, an AI tool announced in 2025 that converts prompts and designs into working prototypes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Figma offers a free Starter tier with limited files and daily AI credits, a Professional tier starting at 16 dollars per month for a Full seat, an annual-only Organization tier at 55 dollars per month per Full seat, and an annual-only Enterprise tier at 90 dollars per month per Full seat.
Pricing is structured around different seat types, Full, Dev, and Collab, at different price points, while Viewer seats for stakeholders who only need to review files remain free across all plans.

features.title: Real-time multiplayer editing
features.description: Multiple users can edit the same file simultaneously with live cursors and instant sync, powered by Rust-based multiplayer servers and a WebAssembly scene graph.

features.title: Vector editing and components
features.description: Professional-grade vector design tools combined with reusable components and variables let teams build and maintain consistent, scalable design systems.

features.title: Auto layout
features.description: Auto layout lets designers build responsive, flexible layouts that automatically adjust as content and screen sizes change, similar to modern CSS flexbox behavior.

features.title: Dev Mode
features.description: A dedicated developer-focused view, launched in 2023, for inspecting design specifications, extracting code snippets, and managing design-to-engineering handoff.

features.title: FigJam whiteboarding
features.description: A real-time collaborative whiteboard product for brainstorming, workshops, and open-ended ideation, extending Figma's multiplayer technology beyond structured design.

features.title: Figma Make
features.description: An AI-powered tool, announced in 2025, that converts text prompts or existing designs into working, interactive prototypes through a conversational interface.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: A community-built plugin and widget ecosystem exceeding 5,000 plugins extends Figma's functionality across design, workflow automation, and content generation.

features.title: Figma Slides and Sites
features.description: Figma Slides supports building presentations and Figma Sites supports publishing web pages, both built directly from the same design files and components.

pros: Pioneering real-time multiplayer collaboration makes team design work dramatically faster than file-based desktop tools
Fully browser-based and cross-platform, requiring no installation and working consistently across operating systems
Extensive plugin ecosystem and Dev Mode significantly streamline design-to-development handoff
Broad product suite covering design, whiteboarding, presentations, and AI-assisted prototyping in one connected platform
cons: Organization and Enterprise tiers require annual billing, which may not suit teams wanting monthly flexibility
Stock has been highly volatile since its 2025 IPO, reflecting broader uncertainty despite the product's strong market position
Full-featured seats are notably more expensive than Dev or Collab seats, which can add up for larger design-heavy teams
Reliance on a constant internet connection, being fully browser-based, can be a limitation in low-connectivity environments

faqs.question: Who founded Figma?
faqs.answer: Figma was founded in 2012 by Dylan Field and Evan Wallace, who met as computer science students at Brown University, with Field leaving school on a Thiel Fellowship to build the company.

faqs.question: Why did Adobe's acquisition of Figma fail?
faqs.answer: Adobe agreed to acquire Figma for approximately 20 billion dollars in September 2022, but the deal was terminated in December 2023 after EU and UK antitrust regulators raised concerns it would eliminate competition, with Adobe paying Figma a 1 billion dollar termination fee.

faqs.question: Is Figma a public company?
faqs.answer: Yes, Figma went public on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol FIG on July 31, 2025, pricing its IPO at 33 dollars per share.

faqs.question: Is Figma free to use?
faqs.answer: Figma offers a free Starter tier with limited files and daily AI credits, alongside paid Professional, Organization, and Enterprise tiers with expanded features and seat types.

faqs.question: What is Dev Mode in Figma?
faqs.answer: Dev Mode is a dedicated view within Figma, launched in 2023, designed for developers to inspect finished designs, extract specifications and code snippets, and implement interfaces accurately based on the design file.

faqs.question: What are Figma's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Figma is most commonly compared to Adobe XD, Sketch, InVision, Canva, Framer, and the open-source alternative Penpot, though Figma remains widely regarded as the default choice for collaborative design teams.

use_cases.description: Design teams use Figma's real-time multiplayer editing to work together on interface designs, components, and design systems without exporting or emailing files.
use_cases.audience: UI/UX designers and product design teams

use_cases.title: Design-to-development handoff
use_cases.description: Developers use Dev Mode to inspect finished designs, extract specifications and code snippets, and implement interfaces accurately based on the design file.

use_cases.description: Cross-functional teams use FigJam's real-time whiteboarding for retrospectives, brainstorming sessions, and workshop facilitation beyond structured interface design.

enrichment.short_description: Figures is a European compensation management platform that helps HR and People teams benchmark salaries, build pay bands, run compensation review cycles, and manage pay equity compliance.
enrichment.seo_title: Figures Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Figures review: a European compensation benchmarking and pay equity platform for HR teams. Explore features, pricing structure, and alternatives like Pave.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Figures is a compensation management platform built for HR and People teams, with a particular focus on the European market and, increasingly, on compliance with the European Union's Pay Transparency Directive. The platform combines several distinct but connected modules: a real-time salary benchmarking tool that compares a company's own compensation data against a broader market dataset, a salary bands or pay grid builder that helps HR teams define structured, defensible compensation ranges for each role and level, structured tooling for running compensation review cycles that includes budget tracking and progress monitoring as managers propose raises across an organization, and pay-equity and pay-gap analysis functionality designed to help companies identify and address unjustified compensation disparities, including specific reporting aligned with the EU Pay Transparency Directive's requirements. To keep its benchmarking data current and connected to a company's actual HR systems, Figures integrates with more than thirty human resources information systems, including widely used platforms such as Workday, HiBob, Personio, SAP, BambooHR, and Charlie HR, allowing compensation data to flow into the platform without extensive manual data entry.

Figures was founded in 2020 by Virgile Raingeard, who serves as CEO, and Bastien Formery, who serves as CTO, and the company is most commonly described as headquartered in Paris, France, though one third-party business database lists the more specific location of Nanterre, a suburb in the greater Paris metropolitan area, and the exact precise city should be treated as not fully confirmed beyond this general Paris-area location. Figures has raised two publicly disclosed funding rounds: a seed round of approximately 1.7 million euros in 2021 led by Seedcamp, followed by a Series A round of approximately 6.67 million euros in September 2022 led by Point Nine, with continued participation from Seedcamp, along with Bpifrance, the French public investment bank, Kima Ventures, and new investors identified as Acadian and Entrée. Combined, these two rounds total approximately 8.37 million euros, a figure broadly consistent with a separate podcast interview in which CEO Virgile Raingeard referenced approximately 8.5 million euros in total funding raised, though that figure comes from a podcast headline reference rather than a formal press release and has not been independently itemized beyond the two rounds already described. It is worth explicitly noting, given the similarity in naming, that an unrelated American company called simply 'Figure,' founded by Miles Hobby and Geoffrey Tisserand and backed by CRV, raised 7.5 million dollars covered by TechCrunch in May 2021 for its own separate compensation management platform; this is a distinct company from figures.hr and should not be conflated with it despite operating in a similar product category around the same time.

Company size estimates for Figures vary somewhat by source: the company's own about page describes a team of '30-plus' members, while a secondary source describes a '40-plus' person remote-first team distributed across Europe; the precise current headcount is not confirmed beyond this general range of roughly thirty to forty employees, consistent with a company that has raised a relatively modest, early-stage amount of venture funding and has grown primarily since its 2020 founding rather than through any major acquisition or aggressive late-stage scaling.

Figures does not publish specific dollar or euro pricing on its public pricing page; instead, pricing is entirely custom and quote-based, determined by a combination of a company's total employee headcount and which specific product modules it chooses to license. The platform's pricing page describes three core plans by name rather than by price: a Structure plan, focused on benchmarking a company's existing salaries against market data; a Companion plan, focused on building and maintaining salary bands and pay grids; and a Pay Transparency plan, focused on supporting collaborative compensation decision-making in line with regulatory transparency requirements. Beyond these three core plans, Figures also offers an add-on module called Pay Equity Expert for companies that need more specialized support analyzing and addressing pay equity gaps. Any company interested in pricing must book a demonstration call with Figures' sales team to receive a specific quote, a common approach among B2B software companies selling into HR and compliance functions where pricing is typically influenced heavily by company size and the complexity of existing compensation structures.

Figures' target customers are HR, People, and Total Rewards or Compensation teams, spanning organizations from early-stage startups and scale-ups through to larger mid-market and, increasingly, enterprise companies, particularly as European regulatory pressure around pay transparency has grown. The company's own materials and third-party sources cite figures ranging from '500-plus companies' on Figures' own about page to a higher claim of '1,200-plus clients, including seven CAC40 companies' from a separate secondary source, referring to companies included in France's benchmark CAC40 stock market index; a LinkedIn product listing separately describes '100-plus companies across 20-plus countries.' These figures diverge meaningfully across sources and likely reflect different points in the company's growth trajectory or different definitions of an active customer, so any single specific customer count should be treated as approximate marketing material rather than an audited figure. Named customers referenced across these various sources include Doctolib, KPMG, TotalEnergies, EY, Qonto, Swile, Malt, Kingfisher, and Duolingo, spanning technology, professional services, energy, and consumer companies, indicating adoption both within France specifically and more broadly across Europe. The size of Figures' underlying benchmarking dataset is similarly described inconsistently across sources, ranging from '3.5 million-plus data points' cited on the company's main site to '500,000-plus European salary benchmarks' cited on a LinkedIn product page, again suggesting these are marketing figures that have evolved over time rather than a single fixed, precisely defined number.

Within its competitive category, Figures is most frequently compared against Pave, a United States-based compensation benchmarking company founded in 2019 by Matt Schulman; Ravio, a London-based compensation platform founded in 2022; and OpenComp, another compensation benchmarking company, with all three grouped together in third-party comparison sources as 'real-time data' compensation platforms that continuously update benchmarking data rather than relying on periodic surveys. Separately, more traditional, survey-based compensation data providers such as PayScale and Salary.com are sometimes referenced as an older generation of alternative, though these operate on a fundamentally different, less real-time data collection model. Figures' primary differentiation within this competitive set, according to third-party analysis, centers on its explicitly European and French-market focus, its real-time rather than periodic-survey approach to benchmarking data, and its specific tooling built around compliance with the EU Pay Transparency Directive, a regulatory requirement that is considerably more central to Figures' product positioning than it is for its more US-centric competitors like Pave and OpenComp. The company holds ISO 27001 certification and, per at least one source, SOC 2 Type 2 certification, along with stated GDPR compliance, credentials that matter considerably to HR software buyers handling sensitive employee compensation data, and the product itself is available in both English and French, reflecting its dual focus on serving both its home French market and the broader English-speaking European and international business community.

From a technical and market-position standpoint, Figures operates in a compensation technology category that has grown substantially as European regulators have pushed pay transparency legislation forward, most notably the EU Pay Transparency Directive, which requires companies operating in the European Union to meet new disclosure and reporting standards around pay gaps and salary ranges; this regulatory backdrop has given Figures a somewhat differentiated growth driver compared to its more US-centric competitors, since demand for its Pay Transparency plan and pay-equity analysis tooling is directly tied to a legal compliance deadline rather than purely to voluntary adoption of better compensation practices. The company's product evolution since its 2020 founding has moved from an initial focus on salary benchmarking data toward a broader suite spanning pay bands, review cycles, and compliance reporting, reflecting a common trajectory for HR technology vendors that start with a single high-value data product and gradually expand into the surrounding workflow once they have established trust with compensation and HR teams. Because compensation data is highly sensitive and compliance-related, Figures' emphasis on certifications such as ISO 27001 and its stated GDPR compliance function as important trust signals for prospective customers evaluating the platform, particularly for larger organizations such as the CAC40-listed companies referenced among its customer base, where procurement processes typically require rigorous security and compliance vetting before any compensation data is shared with an external vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Figures is a Paris-based compensation management platform, founded in 2020 by Virgile Raingeard and Bastien Formery, that helps HR and People teams benchmark salaries, build pay bands, run compensation review cycles, and manage pay equity compliance, with a particular focus on the European market.

The company has raised approximately 8.37 million euros across seed and Series A rounds led by Seedcamp and Point Nine respectively, and serves customers including Doctolib, KPMG, TotalEnergies, and Duolingo.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Figures combines real-time salary benchmarking against market data, a salary bands and pay grid builder, structured compensation review cycle tooling with budget tracking, and pay-equity analysis aligned with the EU Pay Transparency Directive.
The platform integrates with more than thirty HRIS systems, including Workday, HiBob, Personio, SAP, and BambooHR, so compensation data can flow into the platform without extensive manual entry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Figures does not publish fixed pricing; costs are determined by a company's employee headcount and which of its three core plans, Structure, Companion, or Pay Transparency, plus the optional Pay Equity Expert add-on, a company selects.
Prospective customers must book a demonstration call with Figures' sales team to receive a specific quote tailored to their organization's size and needs.

faqs.question: What does Figures do?
faqs.answer: Figures is a compensation management platform that helps HR and People teams benchmark salaries against market data, build salary bands, run compensation review cycles, and analyze pay equity, with strong focus on European compliance requirements.

faqs.question: How much does Figures cost?
faqs.answer: Figures does not publish fixed pricing; costs depend on company headcount and which plan, Structure, Companion, or Pay Transparency, a company selects, and require booking a demo with Figures' sales team for a quote.

faqs.question: Who founded Figures?
faqs.answer: Figures was founded in 2020 by Virgile Raingeard, who serves as CEO, and Bastien Formery, who serves as CTO, and is headquartered in the Paris, France area.

faqs.question: Which HR systems does Figures integrate with?
faqs.answer: Figures integrates with more than thirty HR information systems, including Workday, HiBob, Personio, SAP, BambooHR, and Charlie HR.

faqs.question: Is Figures the same company as Figure, the US compensation platform?
faqs.answer: No, Figures (figures.hr) is a distinct, unrelated company from Figure, a US-based compensation management platform founded by Miles Hobby and Geoffrey Tisserand that raised funding covered by TechCrunch in 2021.

faqs.question: What are Figures' main competitors?
faqs.answer: Figures is most often compared to Pave, Ravio, and OpenComp, other real-time compensation benchmarking platforms, as well as older survey-based providers like PayScale and Salary.com.
